moveAnnotations.annotation: This is the Hungarian Opening, an unusual and underrated approach where we fianchetto our kingside bishop before establishing a strong center. By playing g3, we prepare to develop our bishop to g2, where it will exert powerful control over the long diagonal (a1-h8) and put pressure on Black's center and queenside. This move is less direct than 1.e4 or 1.d4, but it offers flexibility and can lead to interesting strategic battles where we avoid heavily-analyzed mainlines. The Hungarian Opening is particularly popular with players who prefer maneuvering positions over forcing, theoretical continuations.

Black mirrors our fianchetto setup with the Myers Defense, preparing to place their own bishop on g7 along the long diagonal. However, by committing the g-pawn so early and advancing it two squares, Black weakens the kingside considerably—the f5 square becomes a significant outpost for our pieces, and the h5 square is left vulnerable. This move can lead to an imbalanced game where Black's pieces have some activity on the queenside, but White's long diagonal piece (once developed to g2) often dominates. We should be prepared to exploit the weakened kingside by developing efficiently and potentially pushing e4 or d4 when the moment is right.
